feature: bober-postmortemer agent + bober.postmortem skill — automated postmortem synthesis
description: New read-only subagent that reads .bober/incidents/<id>/ artifacts (timeline, observations, hypotheses, actions, changelog, runbook-execution, diagnoses) and produces a structured postmortem document at .bober/incidents/<id>/postmortem.md. Contains 5-Whys analysis, timeline summary, contributing factors, action items. Pure offline synthesis — no live observability access.

successCriteria.description: agents/bober-postmortemer.md exists with YAML frontmatter, read-only tools (Read, Bash, Grep, Glob — no observability MCPs since postmortem is offline), Iron Law / Red Flags / Rationalization-Prevention table per Sprint 3 style.
successCriteria.verificationMethod: Read file. Verify YAML, tool restriction, structural blocks.

successCriteria.description: Iron Law: 'NO POSTMORTEM SECTION WITHOUT EVIDENCE FROM INCIDENT ARTIFACTS' (or equivalent — every claim in the postmortem traces back to a specific incident artifact path).

skills/bober.postmortem/SKILL.md teaches the synthesis discipline: timeline reconstruction from chronological events, 5-Whys from hypotheses + changelog, contributing factors from observations, action items from gaps in process. Includes a template postmortem.md structure.
successCriteria.verificationMethod: Read file. Verify each named section in the discipline.

successCriteria.description: Postmortem template (in skill body): markdown with required sections — Title, Status, Severity, TL;DR, Timeline (table), Impact, Root Cause (with 5-Whys), Contributing Factors, What Went Well, What Went Wrong, Action Items (with owner placeholder + due-date placeholder). Each section's expected content documented.
successCriteria.verificationMethod: Read template section. Verify all sections present.

successCriteria.description: Implementation: src/incident/postmortem.ts exports generatePostmortem(incidentId): Promise<{path, content}>. Spawns bober-postmortemer with the incident artifacts. Validates the output is parseable markdown matching the template.
successCriteria.verificationMethod: Read file. Test: with a fixture incident, generatePostmortem produces a valid postmortem.md.

successCriteria.description: Status linkage: when an incident transitions to status='resolved' (Sprint 22 gates this), the orchestrator OFFERS to generate a postmortem (interactive) or auto-generates (configurable via incident.autoPostmortem default true). incident.json gains postmortemPath field on success.
successCriteria.verificationMethod: Read status-transition wiring. Test: resolved incident → postmortem generated → incident.json updated.

successCriteria.description: Evidence traceability: every claim in the postmortem cites a source via inline reference like '(see timeline.jsonl#L42)' or '(observations.jsonl event-id obs-3)'. Skill body documents the citation format.
successCriteria.verificationMethod: Test: generated postmortem grep for citation markers; count >0.

successCriteria.description: CLI command: `bober postmortem generate <incidentId>` (re-generate even after auto-gen), `bober postmortem show <incidentId>` (cat the postmortem).

generatorNotes:
Postmortems are most valuable when they're EVIDENCE-BASED. The Iron Law about citation backs this — no claim without traceability. Without it, postmortems become opinion documents.

Template (in skill body, ~60-80 lines):
```markdown
# Postmortem: <symptom>

**Incident ID:** <id>  
**Status:** Resolved  
**Severity:** <S1|S2|S3|S4>  
**Date:** <createdAt> → <resolvedAt>  
**Duration:** <hh:mm>

## TL;DR
<2-3 sentence summary — symptom, root cause, fix>

## Impact
- <quantitative impact: requests affected, customers affected, revenue impact, SLO breach>
- <each line cites observations.jsonl or resolution-evidence/>

## Timeline
| Time | Event | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| 14:00 UTC | Error rate spiked to 5% | obs__datadog (timeline.jsonl#L1) |
| 14:05 UTC | On-call paged | alert system (timeline.jsonl#L2) |
| 14:12 UTC | Hypothesis: db slow query | bober-diagnoser (diagnoses/<id>.json) |
| 14:25 UTC | Action: scaled db replicas 3→6 | bober-deployer (changelog.jsonl#L1) |
| 14:38 UTC | Error rate < 0.1% sustained | resolution verify (resolution-evidence/) |

## Root Cause (5-Whys)
1. Why did the error rate spike? — Database queries timing out.
2. Why were queries timing out? — Connection pool exhausted.
3. Why was the pool exhausted? — New checkout flow opens 2x connections per request.
4. Why does it open 2x? — Migration 042 added redundant connection in transaction wrapper.
5. Why did the redundancy ship? — Code review missed the wrapper-inside-wrapper pattern.
(Each Why cites supporting evidence path.)

## Contributing Factors
- <each factor with evidence path>

## What Went Well
- <bullets, each evidence-backed>

## What Went Wrong
- <bullets, each evidence-backed>

## Action Items
| Item | Owner | Due | Source |
|------|-------|-----|--------|
| Add wrapper-pattern check to code-reviewer | TBD | TBD | (5-whys-5) |
| Add SLO alert for connection pool saturation | TBD | TBD | (root-cause) |
```

bober-postmortemer reads artifacts in this order:
1. incident.json for metadata
2. timeline.jsonl for chronological skeleton
3. observations.jsonl for verified facts
4. diagnoses/*.json for hypotheses (selects highest-confidence as root cause candidate)
5. changelog.jsonl for actions taken
6. runbook-execution.jsonl for runbook flow if any
7. resolution-evidence/ for resolution proof

The 5-Whys section is the hardest to synthesize from artifacts — the diagnoser's top hypothesis is usually Why #2 or Why #3, not Why #1. The synthesizer needs to work backwards (symptom → root) and forwards (root → symptom) to fill all five steps. Document this in the skill body.

Auto-postmortem on resolve: hook into Sprint 19's setIncidentStatus. When status transitions to 'resolved' AND incident.autoPostmortem !== false, spawn the synthesizer asynchronously, update incident.json.postmortemPath when done. Don't block the status transition on postmortem generation — postmortems can be slow (model latency).
evaluatorNotes:
Verify evidence citation enforcement: a test that runs generatePostmortem on a fixture incident must produce a postmortem.md where EVERY non-template claim has an inline citation. Grep for citation markers like '(timeline.jsonl' or '(diagnoses/' or '(observations.jsonl'; the count should be >5 for a typical fixture.

Verify the synthesizer is OFFLINE (no observability MCP access). If the agent prompt grants observability tools, that contradicts the design — postmortems should be reproducible from disk artifacts alone for audit purposes. Read agents/bober-postmortemer.md tools field; assert no MCP tools.

Verify auto-postmortem doesn't block status transition: a test that sets status='resolved' should return immediately even if postmortem generation is slow. The postmortemPath field updates asynchronously when synthesis completes.

Verify the template structure is present in generated postmortems: parse the output, assert all section headings exist. A postmortem missing 'Action Items' is incomplete.

Verify 5-Whys section: a generated 5-Whys with fewer than 3 levels signals shallow synthesis — surface a warning. A 5-Whys with 5 levels each citing evidence is the bar.

Check for sensitive data exposure: postmortem.md may end up shared with broader audiences. The synthesizer should NOT include raw secrets/tokens/PII from artifacts. Skill body should explicitly call out redaction discipline. Test with a fixture artifact containing a fake API key — verify the synthesizer redacts it in the output.
